summary: On Kriya-shaped Apolaki tasks, Opus 4.8 and Sonnet 5 are close. Opus is the stronger exhaustive reviewer/gate; Sonnet 5 matches it on design breadth and domain awareness at lower cost. Their defect blind spots are complementary. Effort raises recall but not gate verdicts.

recommendations.rationale: Most exhaustive defect enumeration and fewest misses in both labs. Gate PASS/VETO was identical at default and max effort, so pay for high, not max, on the decision itself.
recommendations.evidence: Task C: 11 findings @default, 14 @max, correct VETO both times; Task A: 8-9 defects vs Sonnet's 6-7.

constraints: cap output tokens — 1.5-1.9x more verbose than Opus at matched effort
recommendations.rationale: Matched Opus on rubric coverage with richer real-world/domain modeling; cheaper per task (esp. intro pricing). Its verbosity is the thing to control.
recommendations.evidence: Task B: 14/14 rubric both; Sonnet added PH-specific edges (co-op/prepaid meters, Tagalog OCR, fraud-via-edit, vendor DPA).

recommendations.rationale: Tighter, more minimal output and a data-minimization instinct; caught more brownfield gotchas (incl. null-safety, brittle checks) in fewer tokens.
recommendations.evidence: Task A: Opus found the most valid defects with lower verbosity.

recommendations.kria_stage: drafts / first-pass triage / breadth sweeps (low-stakes, non-gate)
recommendations.rationale: Cost-efficient and fast enough with adequate recall for non-gate work; escalate to Opus for the gate.
recommendations.evidence: Default effort already produced correct verdicts and most core defects at ~5% (std) / 37% (intro) lower cost.

recommendations.rationale: Blind spots are complementary and effort does NOT close them: at max effort Opus still missed the stateful-regex bug Sonnet caught. For critical audits, union both models' findings rather than trusting one.
recommendations.evidence: Task C: Sonnet uniquely caught stateful g/y regex nondeterminism at both effort levels; Opus never did, even at max.

principles: Pay for max effort on ENUMERATION (audits, exhaustive review), not on binary PASS/VETO — verdicts were effort-robust.
Cap Sonnet 5 output; its cheaper tokens only pay off once verbosity (1.5-1.9x) is controlled.
For the highest-stakes reviews, ensemble beats any single model — blind spots are model-characteristic, not effort-limited.
Front-load Sonnet-heavy work before 2026-08-31 while intro pricing ($2/$10) makes it ~37-40% cheaper.
caveats: Single run per task — signal, not a benchmark.
Graded against published answer keys by the author, not a blind expert panel.
Effort held EQUAL across arms (identical directive), not pinned to a literal 'max' knob.
Sonnet 5 was < 2 days old at test time; behaviour/routing still settling.
Costs normalized to official published rates, not Claude Code's internal cost table.
